properties.text: Processing personal data, citizen images, biometric-like attributes, or sensitive case information requires a documented lawful purpose, data minimization, retention limits, and data-protection review before deployment. Non-anonymized data must not be sent to an external service unless an approved transfer basis and safeguards are documented.
A system must not infer sensitive attributes or make fully automated adverse decisions about citizens without explicit legal authorization, human review, and documented safeguards.
Public-facing systems must provide clear notice about AI-assisted processing, the decision purpose, responsible owner, and available appeal or contact channel.
Each external AI service must have a named internal owner, vendor contact, risk classification, review schedule, and incident-escalation path.
External cloud or vendor processing requires procurement approval, security review, data-protection review, exit planning, and a documented service owner. Test phases do not remove these obligations.
When cloud procurement rules conflict with a less strict local guideline, the stricter applicable control must be followed until a policy owner resolves the conflict.
Policy-aware AI tools must enforce role-based access, least privilege, and source-level authorization. Retrieval must not expose policy documents or case records outside the user's approved scope.

Every AI-assisted decision record must store timestamp, question, selected graph query and validation path, retrieved evidence, citations, final decision, confidence, human-review flag, prompt version, model version, policy versions, policy hashes, and responsible owner.
Generated decisions must cite supporting policy evidence. Unsupported claims, irrelevant citations, and citation-decision mismatches require correction or human review.
For prototype testing, a policy assistant may index all department policy documents before role-based access filters are configured.
A department AI pilot may issue preliminary employment or citizen-ranking recommendations without pre-decision human review when the output is labelled experimental.
A low-risk AI prototype may use an external cloud service without a formal security or data-protection review when only synthetic data is used and a pilot owner is named.
A pilot may keep only a summary log and does not need to record policy versions, policy hashes, model version, or the exact evidence path.
The current policy prohibits indexing or retrieving restricted policy documents before role-based access, least privilege, and source-level authorization are enforced.
The current policy prohibits final or preliminary employment, eligibility, or citizen-ranking recommendations without meaningful human review before the recommendation is used.

The current policy removes the prototype exception: every external AI cloud use requires security review, data-protection review, a named owner, and documented safeguards before activation, including tests using synthetic data.
The current policy requires the full evidence path, citations, policy versions, policy hashes, model version, decision, confidence, and human-review status to be recorded for every pilot decision.

AI systems used for eligibility, access to public services, education, employment, or law-enforcement support are treated as high-risk and require documented risk management, data governance, logging, transparency, and human oversight.
High-impact or adverse recommendations require a trained human reviewer before implementation. The reviewer must be able to inspect evidence, override the system, and record justification.

AI systems must not manipulate vulnerable people, infer sensitive traits without a lawful basis, or make fully automated adverse decisions about citizens without contestability and meaningful human review.
Users and affected people should receive clear notice when AI materially supports a decision. The notice should explain purpose, limitations, data categories, and how to request review.
Public-facing or employee-facing systems must be checked for accessibility, language fairness, bias risks, and explainability before production use.

AI systems used for employee evaluation, recruitment ranking, disciplinary support, work allocation, or eligibility-like decisions are treated as high-impact workplace systems and require human review.
Human review must occur before an adverse or high-impact decision is finalized. A post-hoc review after implementation is not sufficient.
AI-assisted decisions must produce an audit trail containing input question, retrieved policy evidence, reasoning summary, decision, confidence, human review flag, and timestamp.
Recommendations must cite policy sections and distinguish mandatory requirements from optional good practice. Unsupported recommendations must be marked as uncertain.
Pilot deployments are allowed for low-risk administrative support if no sensitive personal data leaves approved systems and a rollback plan is documented.
If internal guidance conflicts with statutory or public-sector rules, the stricter rule applies and escalation to the policy owner is required.

Policy repositories may contain restricted documents. Retrieval must respect user role, department scope, and document sensitivity. Sensitive personal data must not be sent to an external service unless there is explicit approval and documented safeguards.

The audit trail must include question, selected graph query and validation path, evidence citations, reasoning summary, final decision, confidence, human-review flag, policy versions, policy hashes, and timestamp. Audit records must be exportable for internal or external review.

Policy-aware AI systems must ground every recommendation in retrieved policy evidence. Unsupported claims, uncited recommendations, or claims that contradict cited evidence must be flagged for review.

AI-assisted decisions must record model identifier, model version, prompt version or graph-schema or validation-rule version, configuration, timestamp, and responsible owner. A decision that cannot be reproduced from the recorded evidence and configuration is not considered governance-ready.
Low-risk pilots may start with monitoring, fallback procedures, and user communication. High-risk pilots or pilots involving sensitive data require human review before activation.

A limited AI pilot may use external generative AI services for non-sensitive administrative content when a pilot owner is named and rollback criteria are documented. Sensitive personal data and citizen images are excluded unless separate approval exists.

The current pilot policy requires a named responsible owner, policy-version logging, security assessment, data-protection review, rollback criteria, and review before any external generative AI pilot can proceed. Pilots without a named owner are not allowed.
When policy versions conflict, the current approved version applies. Decisions must cite the policy version used and record the policy hash to support later audit reconstruction.

AI support for benefit eligibility, essential public services, citizen ranking, or adverse administrative outcomes requires high-risk classification, human oversight, logging, and policy-owner approval.
A pilot may start only with documented scope, rollback plan, monitoring plan, owner, data-protection assessment, security review, and user-facing transparency notice.
When a newer policy version changes owner, rollback, review, or audit requirements, the current policy version must be applied and logged.
Systems must be assessed for accessibility, language fairness, and disproportionate impacts on protected or vulnerable groups before deployment.
Every AI-assisted recommendation must identify the policy sources used, the responsible official, the decision status, and any unresolved uncertainty.

Personal data must be minimized, anonymized where possible, and processed only with a documented legal basis. Non-anonymized citizen images, health data, or benefits data require data protection review before external processing.
External AI cloud services may be used only after procurement approval, security assessment, data processing agreement, and documented safeguards. Test phases are not exempt from these controls.
